evidence: All four api-layer classes import com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange directly; HttpApiUtils static helpers accept HttpExchange, binding the utility layer to a JDK-internal type
WordSketchApiServer.registerHandler receives com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer as a parameter, leaking the internal type into the registration API
RelationType and PosGroup are in the config package but are consumed by query, api, and exploration packages — acting as a shared vocabulary that blurs config vs domain boundaries
Packages (api, query, config, exploration, model, indexer, utils, viz) map cleanly to functional roles with no obvious cycles detected
LongIntHashMap in utils is a standalone data-structure utility with no upward dependencies
Package structure (api, query, config, exploration, model, indexer, utils, viz) maps sensibly to domain capabilities
model package uses Java records consistently for AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, ExploreOptions, ExplorationResult, Edge, etc.
Some classes use regular class syntax (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun) while peers use records, creating minor convention inconsistency
RelationType and PosGroup enums sit in config package but are domain vocabulary consumed across api, query, and exploration — ownership is ambiguous
GrammarConfigLoader is a loader that also holds runtime query state (relationsById map, getRelation accessor) — loader vs repository boundary is blurred
ExploreOptions Javadoc acknowledges 'Explore vs Exploration naming asymmetry is historical' — documented technical debt
requireNextArg has two consecutive Javadoc blocks — a draft version immediately followed by a fuller replacement — violating the one-comment-per-member convention

BlackLabSnippetParser class uses get-prefixed accessor style (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch) in some methods but plain-verb style (findLabelPosition, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition) in others

QueryResults.WordSketchResult uses record accessor syntax (result.lemma(), result.frequency()) while some helper builders use Map.put string keys — accessor style is inconsistent at the usage boundary

BlackLabQueryExecutor uses a private static String method (buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution) while sibling logic in RelationConfig uses an instance method (getFullPattern) for the same pattern-substitution concern
Codebase is otherwise consistently camelCase Java; no snake_case drift or mixed capitalization detected

handleBcqlQueryPost has two catch blocks: IOException sends 500 with 'BCQL query failed', but Exception sends 400 with 'BCQL query error' — conflating client error (400) with any unclassified runtime exception is inconsistent

BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution throws IllegalArgumentException for bad pattern format; the caller (findCollocations) wraps it in IOException — IllegalArgumentException is a programming error, not an I/O failure
handleSemanticFieldExplore catches IOException and sends 500 but also catches IllegalArgumentException for 400; this is a correct distinction and is consistent across exploration handlers
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency wraps RuntimeException in IOException with a descriptive message — this is consistent with the pattern used elsewhere in the executor
ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks uses a null-flag pattern for the writer (writer==null signals 'not open') instead of an Optional or state object, which slightly obscures the lifecycle logic
wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer catches both IOException (500) and Exception (500) uniformly, providing a safe backstop

LEMMA_ATTR and LEMMA_ATTR_ANY in BlackLabSnippetParser have a subtle behavioural difference (the latter is case-insensitive and accepts single quotes) but their names imply only a specificity difference ('any' suggesting broader match), not a quote-style difference

findLabelPosition returns the 1-based token position within a CQL pattern, but 'position' in a CQL context could mean character offset, token index, or label number — the return semantics are ambiguous from the name alone
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution is accurate but verbose; sibling method names (getFullPattern, substituteAtPosition) use shorter domain vocabulary
ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed is described in Javadoc as relevant only when used with single-seed exploration — the name does not signal this conditionality
Overall naming quality is high: domain terms (lemma, collocate, deprel, logDice, headword) are used consistently and correctly throughout

QueryExecutor interface has two implementations: BlackLabQueryExecutor (production) and presumably a test stub — single-implementation risk is low given the interface has real contract value for testing

CollocateQueryHelper is a package-private class used only by BlackLabQueryExecutor — it adds a level of indirection that is justified by the extracted complexity of collocate ranking and frequency lookup

handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType in SketchHandlers are structurally near-identical (both iterate grammarConfig.getRelations(), catch errors, build response) but cannot be cleanly unified because the response shape differs (surface returns collocations list, dep returns full buildRelationResponse) — the abstraction gap is real
ExploreOptions bundles nullable/conditional parameters (nounsPerSeed is unused in multi-seed mode) — a wide options bag for a single method with implicit conditionality
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern takes RelationConfig as a parameter, passing a config object into an exploration domain class — the abstraction boundary leaks config into exploration logic
HttpApiUtils is a static utility class with no state — appropriate for the domain; no pass-through wrapper smell

com.sun.net.httpserver is a JDK-internal API (sun.* hierarchy) used pervasively across api package; not a Maven dependency but an implicit platform dependency that is not guaranteed stable across JDK versions

fastjson2 (com.alibaba.fastjson2) is used for JSON serialisation/deserialisation — an unconventional choice over Jackson or Gson in the Java ecosystem; increases onboarding friction for contributors expecting standard libraries
BlackLab (nl.inl.blacklab) is the corpus search engine — the primary heavy dependency with clear justification for its weight given the search functionality it enables
slf4j-api is correctly used for logging with no direct log4j/log4j2 imports visible in source
jspecify annotations (@Nullable) are imported in RelationConfig and BlackLabSnippetParser — a lightweight nullability annotation library, appropriate use
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose detected (no moment+dayjs equivalent, no multiple JSON libraries — fastjson2 is used exclusively)
pom.xml not read directly but CLAUDE.md lists: Lucene 10.3.2, fastjson2 2.0.25, SLF4J 2.0.7, JUnit 5.9.0 — lean dependency set

ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks uses a mutable null-flag pattern for the writer (writer==null meaning 'not yet opened') across a complex nested try-finally; a state object or a factory method would make lifecycle clearer

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern walks the hit groups twice conceptually: once in collectFrequenciesFromGroups (via a separate helper) and the collocatePos calculation from findLabelPosition precedes the search — the double-cursor walk is subtle
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates passes a nullable posMap parameter; callers pass null explicitly in several paths, spreading nullable handling across the call site

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaWithFallback has an implicit assumption that the plain-text fallback (last whitespace-separated token) returns a sensible lemma — this heuristic is not guarded or documented as a last-resort fallback
handleRelationsForType in SketchHandlers constructs a Map<String, Object> per relation with string keys — the response shape is implicit in the handler body rather than expressed as a typed record
ConlluConverter finally block correctly closes the writer, and the method overall handles EOF-without-newline correctly

handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType in SketchHandlers duplicate the outer iteration loop (grammarConfig.getRelations(), relationType check, executeAndFormatCollocations call, catch block, response envelope construction) — a change to error propagation or response shape must be made in both methods

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti share the resolveRelationConfig/resolveExploreParams/buildBaseExploreResponse pattern but diverge in seed parsing — the shared boundary logic is extracted (good) but the two methods are structurally coupled by comments ('nouns_per intentionally not supported in multi-seed mode')
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias provides a clean, single-place alias → canonical-ID translation — a good boundary seam
GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation returns Optional<RelationConfig> cleanly, forcing callers to handle absence explicitly

BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectFrequenciesFromGroups successfully extracts the HitGroups iteration pattern, but executeSurfacePattern bypasses it and inlines its own loop — a mid-level seam inconsistency

requireNextArg in Main.java has two consecutive Javadoc blocks — a short draft ('Returns args[i], exiting with a helpful message...') immediately followed by a longer replacement ('Returns the next argument from args at index i...') — classic AI-generated draft artifact

Several Javadoc comments restate the obvious: QueryResults private constructor 'Utility class - prevent instantiation' on a record-like class; LogDiceCalculator class doc 'Utility class for computing association scores' followed by a 'Supported measures' list that the method signatures already communicate
WordSketchResult.UNKNOWN_POS constant has a Javadoc 'Sentinel for missing POS information returned by the tagger' — the field name already communicates this; the comment adds no new information
handleFullDependencySketch Javadoc: 'Handle full dependency sketch request. Returns all dependency relations for the given lemma.' — this restates the method name verbatim
No noisy debug entry/exit logging detected; logger.debug calls are selective and purposeful
No null-check sprawl on typed non-nullable values detected beyond the nullable parameters that are genuinely optional
GrammarConfigLoader.java validateNoLegacyKeys() actively rejects 'copulas' key with a helpful error — this is a forward-facing migration guard, not a shim that still calls old code
GrammarConfigLoader.java line 158: comment 'cql_pattern is no longer supported' — the old field name is rejected outright rather than silently adapted
RelationConfig.java lines 112, 154: 'legacy format' tag= attribute support coexists with xpos= in posGroupFromConstraint() and posGroupForPrefix()
BlackLabConllUIndexer.java has public indexDirectory() method that is never called from Main.java — visible via grep: only definition, no callsite in main sources
All CLI commands are 'blacklab-*' prefixed, implying a prior non-BlackLab backend, but no old executor classes remain in the codebase
No @deprecated annotations, no DEPRECATED markers, no TODO/FIXME referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after'
api/ contains WordSketchApiServer, SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, HttpApiUtils, RelationUtils — all HTTP-layer concerns, well scoped
config/ contains GrammarConfigLoader, RelationConfig, PosGroup, RelationType — configuration domain, well scoped
exploration/ contains SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator — exploration logic, well scoped
query/ contains BlackLabQueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, QueryExecutor interface, QueryResults — query execution domain

model/ is a catch-all mixing result DTOs (QueryResults, ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult), visualization graph model (Edge, RelationEdgeType), and tuning config (ExploreOptions), and collocate profiles (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun)
utils/ mixes corpus-specific logic (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator) with a generic data structure (LongIntHashMap)
QueryResults is in model/ but imported in query/ classes — it straddles two packages conceptually
RelationConfig record in config/ has substantial query-generation logic (getFullPattern, collocateReversePattern, collocatePosGroup, getDeprel) that goes beyond pure configuration
HttpApiUtils.requireParam() has return type String but can return null when the parameter is missing; Javadoc says 'Caller must check for null return' but no @Nullable annotation — the signature lies
ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig() returns RelationConfig but returns null on failure — no @Nullable in signature
ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams() returns ExploreParams (a record) but returns null on validation failure — no @Nullable in signature

Main.requireNextArg() has two Javadoc comments block (a legacy one and a replacement) — the first block states 'exiting with a helpful message' and the second repeats and elaborates the contract; duplicate javadoc is an incomplete migration artefact

GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader(Reader) closes the Reader it receives inside try(reader) — the Javadoc example shows the caller using try-with-resources on the reader, suggesting the caller also expects to close it, creating a potential double-close if the reader is a resource the caller needs to keep open

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern() guard: 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) return ExplorationResult.empty(seed)' — passes null to ExplorationResult.empty() when seed is null; the method is not documented as nullable-seed-tolerant
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() throws IllegalStateException when relationType() is empty, matching callers that check first — contract is sound but undocumented in method signature

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore() calls resolvedConfig.relationType().orElseThrow() after resolveRelationConfig() already verifies presence — double-checking a guaranteed invariant without documentation

ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks(): inner finally block contains 'try (BufferedWriter toClose = writer) { chunks++; }' — incrementing the chunks counter inside a resource-close expression mixes side effect with cleanup

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits(): 'int limit = Math.min(maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, records.size())' — no (long) cast before multiplication; if maxResults is large, maxResults * 3 overflows int before comparison. Compare with executeBcqlQuery() which correctly uses '(long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR' for the sampleSize calculation

Main.handleBlackLabIndexCommand() calls indexer.indexFile(wplTempDir.toString()) where wplTempDir is a directory path — the method is named indexFile but is passed a directory; it works because BlackLab Indexer.index() handles both, but the naming creates a false impression

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(): 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) return ExplorationResult.empty(seed)' — passing null to empty(seed) when seed is null; the empty() factory stores the seed field as the key, so a null seed produces a result with null seed field, propagating the null downstream

BlackLabSnippetParser.findLabelPosition(): when the label is found but the following character is not '[', the function silently returns -1 without logging — callers receive -1 without knowing why the label was ignored

BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution(): throws IllegalArgumentException for non-'[' patterns with message 'Unrecognized CQL pattern format' but there is only one valid format — the else branch is effectively dead except as a defensive guard
Java provides compile-time type safety; most type safety concerns are Java-specific: missing @Nullable annotations and inconsistent nullability contracts
BlackLabSnippetParser correctly uses @org.jspecify.annotations.Nullable on extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractHeadword, extractPlainTextTokenAt
RelationConfig.getDeprel() correctly annotated @org.jspecify.annotations.Nullable
HttpApiUtils.requireParam() returns 'String' (implicitly non-null) but can return null — missing @Nullable annotation at a public API boundary
ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig() returns 'RelationConfig' but can return null — missing @Nullable
ExplorationHandlers.resolveExploreParams() returns 'ExploreParams' (a record) but can return null — missing @Nullable
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates() accepts Map<String, String> posMap which is passed as null by executeSurfacePattern() — parameter lacks @Nullable annotation
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand() creates 'QueryExecutor executor = new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath)' assigned to interface type — this is correct and typical Java widening
ExplorationResult stores seed as String without nullability constraint — can hold null from exploreByPattern guard
Consistent use of Optional<RelationType> for nullable relationType throughout RelationConfig — good design

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern() contains a custom for-loop over HitGroups that duplicates the logic of collectFrequenciesFromGroups() — the collectFrequenciesFromGroups utility exists precisely for this, but executeSurfacePattern() bypasses it with slightly different extraction logic

Both BlackLabQueryExecutor and CollocateQueryHelper have similar import blocks and share the CorpusQueryLanguageParser / ContextualQueryLanguageParser pattern; the boilerplate flagged by the mechanical detector is at the import/bootstrap level (both files set up a query parse chain with the same structure)

ExplorationHandlers methods (handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison, handleSemanticFieldExamples) each repeat the same parameter-parsing preamble: parseQueryParams, requireParam, resolveRelationConfig, resolveExploreParams

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType() and handleDepSketchForType() have identical structure: iterate relations, try executeAndFormatCollocations, collect errors, build response map — unified only at the collocate-execution level, not at the response-building level

SemanticFieldExplorer mixes two distinct algorithms: single-seed exploration (multi-phase, finds discovered nouns) and multi-seed comparison (fetches per-seed collocates, intersects) — these have different data flows but share a class
RadialPlot.toSVG() is ~100 lines of SVG string building but has single coherent responsibility — acceptable
BlackLabSnippetParser compiles 8 static Pattern constants at class-load time — no I/O, no side effects, appropriate
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is a static final Map.of() — immutable, no side effects at class load

HttpApiUtils.CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = 'http://localhost:3000' is a static final String — hardcoded, comment says 'Restrict to specific origins in production' but there is no mechanism to override it without recompilation; not a boot-order issue but a configurability defect

SemanticFieldExplorer.NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT = '[xpos="NN.*"]' is a static constant that hardcodes English Penn Treebank grammar tag at class level — CollocateProfileComparator.ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = '[xpos="JJ.*"]' similarly hardcoded; these are implicit grammar assumptions baked into static state

Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand(): uses 'WordSketchApiServer[] serverHolder = new WordSketchApiServer[1]' array as mutable holder in shutdown hook — pattern works but is less clear than AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer>

WordSketchApiServer constructor calls HttpServer.create() (binds port) AND registers routes — if route registration throws after port bind, the port is held until GC; the shutdown hook is registered before server construction so it won't clean up an incomplete construction
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at call time, not at class load — acceptable for a CLI factory method
No circular init dependencies visible across any module
No DB connections, network calls, or file I/O at class-load time (class-level fields are all literals or Pattern.compile() calls)

Tests present for: CqlUtilsTest, LogDiceCalculatorTest, LongIntHashMapTest, HttpApiUtilsTest, RelationUtilsTest, PatternSubstitutionTest, GrammarConfigLoaderTest, RelationsValidationTest, BCQLParserTest, BlackLabSnippetParserTest, CollocateQueryHelperTest, BlackLabQueryExecutorTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, DependencySketchTest, SemanticFieldExplorerTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest, HandlersTest
No test found for BlackLabConllUIndexer — the indexer contains complex error-handling logic (IndexListener with error continuation, close ordering, document/token counting) that is untested

No test found for ConlluConverter — the converter has non-trivial logic: MWT token skipping (regex), nested try/finally with counter side-effect in close, sentence boundary detection; correctness matters for data quality
No test found for WordSketchApiServer route registration — the server constructor wires routes using context paths; a misconfigured prefix could silently misdirect requests
No test found for RadialPlot SVG generation — the toSVG() method has coordinate math and conditional rendering logic

No test found for Main CLI argument parsing — the CLI dispatch logic (handleBlackLabIndexCommand, handleBlackLabQueryCommand, handleBlackLabServerCommand) with edge cases like missing --input, invalid port, etc.
SemanticFieldExplorerTest and CollocateProfileComparatorTest exist — the most complex exploration logic is covered
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest and BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest exist — integration-level query execution is covered
GrammarConfigLoaderTest and RelationsValidationTest provide good coverage of config parsing and validation

GET /api/semantic-field/explore uses 'seed' (singular) while GET /api/semantic-field/explore-multi uses 'seeds' (plural comma-list) and GET /api/semantic-field uses 'seeds' — three endpoints for related operations use two different parameter names
'nouns_per' is available in single-seed explore but explicitly excluded from multi-seed explore with a code comment — asymmetric API surface for similar endpoints
CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = 'http://localhost:3000' is hardcoded — not configurable, breaks for any deployment with a different frontend origin
CLI parameter is '--limit' but API parameter is 'top'; CLI uses '--min-logdice' while API uses 'min_logdice' — consistent snake_case in API but divergent naming with CLI

/api/sketch/{lemma} response has 'relations' as a map keyed by relation ID; /api/semantic-field/explore response has 'seed_collocates' as a list — different shapes for similarly structured collocation data
/api/bcql is POST-only while all other query endpoints are GET — appropriate for BCQL body size, but not documented in the class-level Javadoc endpoint listing's format notes
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc endpoint listing does not document query parameters, required vs optional params, or error response shapes for any endpoint

QueryExecutor interface has excellent per-method Javadoc; REST endpoint Javadoc in handler methods (e.g., handleSemanticFieldExplore) documents params inline but only at source level, not discoverable by API consumers

HttpApiUtils.requireParam sends 400 for missing params; NumberFormatException in param parsing also sends 400 via resolveExploreParams — error handling is consistent within endpoints but the error messages vary in specificity
WordSketchApiServer binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — only accepts connections from 127.0.0.1, providing network-level access control
CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = 'http://localhost:3000' — restricts browser-origin access to a single local origin
No authentication, no rate limiting, and no access control on any endpoint — the pattern is consistent: all endpoints are equally open
/api/bcql accepts arbitrary BCQL pattern strings via POST — this is the most powerful endpoint (arbitrary corpus queries) and has no input validation beyond BCQL parse errors
HttpApiUtils.requireParam() checks for empty/missing but not for injection or length limits — lemma values are passed directly into BCQL pattern strings via string formatting
CqlUtils.escapeForRegex() escapes backslash and double-quote in lemma values before embedding in BCQL strings — lemma injection is partially mitigated
No audit logging on any endpoint — access to the /api/bcql arbitrary-query endpoint is unlogged
Health endpoint /health is unauthenticated — appropriate and intentional for a status check
All endpoints treated identically — no escalation of privilege or mixed trust boundaries

strengths: Package decomposition maps directly to functional areas (api, query, config, exploration, model, indexer, utils, viz) with no detected import cycles
QueryExecutor interface provides a clean seam between the api layer and the BlackLab implementation
Model types (QueryResults, ExploreOptions, ExplorationResult) are isolated in their own package with no back-dependencies
Main.java orchestrates subsystem wiring at the entry point without letting subsystems reach across each other
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The main architectural weakness is pervasive coupling to com.sun.net.httpserver throughout the api package, combined with config-layer enums (RelationType, PosGroup) acting as cross-cutting vocabulary rather than true config-only concerns.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase scores well because layering is clean and cycles are absent — a developer navigating the codebase can reason about ownership without surprises. The score does not reach the 85+ tier because the JDK-internal HTTP coupling makes the api layer effectively untestable without a live HTTP server, and config enums carrying domain semantics create a mild boundary ambiguity. These are real but bounded issues consistent with a high-70s to low-80s placement on the global anchor scale.

strengths: Top-level package decomposition clearly maps api, query, config, exploration, model, indexer, and utils to distinct domain roles
Consistent use of Java records for value types in the model package (WordSketchResult, ExploreOptions, CollocateResult, RelationConfig, Edge, CoreCollocate)
QueryExecutor interface provides a clean public surface with well-documented method distinctions
README and CLAUDE.md documentation accurately describe the deployed architecture and command structure
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The primary pattern of issues is ownership ambiguity at package boundaries: config leaks domain enums, some model types use class instead of record for no clear reason, and GrammarConfigLoader mixes loading with runtime querying responsibility.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase achieves solid high-level decomposition that a new engineer can navigate quickly, placing it clearly above the median. The score falls short of the 85+ tier because two packages (config, model) have mixed conventions and blurred ownership: config hosts domain vocabulary that belongs in model, and GrammarConfigLoader conflates loading with runtime state access. These are not severe but they are real friction points for change locality.

strengths: Codebase follows standard Java camelCase naming throughout with no mixed-style outliers at package or type level
Record vs class usage is consistent within the model package
Static utility classes (HttpApiUtils, RelationUtils, CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator) follow a uniform package-private or public-final pattern
convention_outlier.issue_character: Convention drift is localized to doc-comment duplication on a single method and a naming-style split within BlackLabSnippetParser rather than a systemic codebase-wide divergence.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is well-behaved by Java convention standards with no naming-scheme collisions or style-island packages. The score does not reach 85 because of the double-Javadoc on requireNextArg (a clear artifact of unresolved edit history) and the get-prefix vs plain-verb split in BlackLabSnippetParser that signals two different patterns were applied at different times without reconciliation.

strengths: wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer provides a uniform 500-backstop for all handler exceptions, preventing unhandled exceptions from escaping to the JDK HTTP server
Exploration handlers correctly distinguish IOException (500) from IllegalArgumentException (400), signalling intentional error typing at boundaries
CollocateQueryHelper wraps RuntimeException in IOException with explanatory messages, preserving context at the subsystem boundary
requireNextArg terminates the JVM with a descriptive message on missing CLI args — acceptable pattern for a CLI main path and consistent across all option handlers
error_consistency.issue_character: The main issue is a status-code assignment error in handleBcqlQueryPost where unclassified runtime exceptions are sent as 400 (client error) rather than 500 (server error), and IllegalArgumentException being wrapped in IOException in findCollocations rather than being treated as a programming error.
error_consistency.score_rationale: Error handling is largely consistent with correct context preservation across the codebase. The score sits in the high-70s because the BCQL handler's 400-for-all-exceptions pattern is a real correctness issue (it misleads API clients), and the IllegalArgumentException-wrapped-in-IOException in the executor conflates programming errors with I/O failures. Neither issue is systemic but both are real.

strengths: Domain vocabulary (lemma, collocate, deprel, headword, logDice, posGroup) is used consistently across all packages with no synonym drift
Record component names (WordSketchResult.frequency, CollocateResult.logDice, ExploreOptions.minLogDice) accurately describe their semantics
RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias clearly communicates its aliasing purpose
Package-level names are descriptive and non-generic (exploration, indexer, viz — not helper, util2, common)
naming_quality.issue_character: Naming issues are isolated to a small set of constants and a method return value where the name understates an important behavioural distinction or leaves return semantics ambiguous.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Naming across the codebase is consistently strong, earning a score well above average. The deduction from the high-80s range comes from LEMMA_ATTR_ANY whose name understates the quote-style difference (a real trap for future callers) and findLabelPosition whose return type (1-based token position) is non-obvious from the method name alone. Neither issue is severe but both would slow a developer reading the code for the first time.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface provides genuine leverage: it decouples the api layer from BlackLab and enables test doubles
CollocateQueryHelper extraction is justified — it encapsulates ~150 lines of ranking and scoring logic that would otherwise bloat BlackLabQueryExecutor
RecordConfig record with rich accessors (getFullPattern, collocatePosGroup, getDeprel) gives callers a clean interface to the grammar model without raw JSON access
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The main abstraction issues are a wide nullable-parameter options bag (ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed unused in multi-seed mode) and near-duplicate dispatch paths in SketchHandlers for surface vs dependency sketches that share structure but cannot be cleanly unified due to response shape differences.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Abstractions in this codebase mostly pay for themselves: the QueryExecutor interface, CollocateQueryHelper, and RelationConfig record all provide real leverage. The score falls into the high-70s because ExploreOptions carries a conditional parameter that should be Optional or moved to a subtype, and the handleFullSketchForType/handleDepSketchForType duplication signals that the response-shape difference has not been captured in the type system.

strengths: Dependency set is lean with no duplicate libraries for the same purpose
BlackLab dependency is fully justified by the corpus search requirements of the domain
SLF4J used correctly as a logging facade with no direct logging implementation imports in source
jspecify used for precise @Nullable annotations — a lightweight and appropriate choice
dependency_health.issue_character: The two dependency health concerns are the JDK-internal HTTP server (com.sun.net.httpserver) which is a platform coupling rather than a versioned library, and fastjson2 which is an unconventional JSON choice that may surprise contributors.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is small and purposeful with no redundant libraries — a strong positive. The score stays in the low-80s rather than reaching 90+ because of two bounded but real concerns: com.sun.net.httpserver is not a stable public API and its use is pervasive, and fastjson2 is an unusual choice that carries both an onboarding cost and some historical security baggage (though fastjson2 is a distinct rewrite from fastjson 1.x with fewer CVEs).

strengths: ConlluConverter correctly handles MWT/empty nodes via a compiled Pattern.compile at class load time, avoiding repeated compilation
collectFrequenciesFromGroups in BlackLabQueryExecutor cleanly extracts the HitGroups iteration into a reusable helper that takes a Function<String,String> lemma extractor
LogDiceCalculator.compute is direct, handles edge cases with documented UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel rather than silent 0 or exception
RelationConfig.posGroupForPrefix is a readable linear scan through a known fixed set without defensive sprawl
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Low-level elegance issues cluster around nullable parameters passed explicitly, null-flag lifecycle state for I/O resources, and heuristic fallbacks that are not well-guarded, rather than complex control flow tangles.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Function-level logic is generally clear and direct, with good extraction level — the codebase avoids both monolith methods and micro-fragmentation. The score stays in the high-70s because ConlluConverter's null-flag writer pattern makes the lifecycle harder to follow than necessary, and the nullable posMap in CollocateQueryHelper spreads null-handling across callers rather than expressing the optionality at the call site.

strengths: RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias is a clean, single-point boundary translation that prevents alias resolution from spreading into handler logic
GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation returning Optional<RelationConfig> forces explicit absence handling at every boundary
ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig and resolveExploreParams are extracted helpers that keep the handler methods focused on orchestration
wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer provides a uniform error propagation boundary for all handlers
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The primary mid-level issue is the handleFullSketchForType/handleDepSketchForType duplication in SketchHandlers, where the iteration and response-envelope logic should be shared but currently forces parallel maintenance of two near-identical orchestration loops.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase handles most integration seams cleanly — boundary translation is localised, Optional is used correctly for absent values, and handler helpers are extracted. The score sits in the high-70s because the SketchHandlers dual-path duplication is a real mid-level elegance failure that forces parallel maintenance, and executeSurfacePattern bypasses the shared HitGroups iteration helper used by other executor methods.

strengths: Debug logging is selective and targeted — no entry/exit logging on every method
No generic names (handleData, processItem) in domain code — domain vocabulary is used consistently
No null-check sprawl on typed values that are known non-null at their call sites
No pass-through wrapper functions with no added logic detected
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: AI-generated debt is limited to Javadoc redundancy: one double-draft comment block and a handful of Javadoc comments that restate the code without adding insight, rather than systemic boilerplate or defensive overengineering.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase shows only modest AI-generated debt — no defensive sprawl, no entry/exit logging, no generic domain names. The score does not reach 90+ because the double-Javadoc on requireNextArg is a clear unresolved draft artifact, and several Javadoc blocks across the codebase restate method names without adding insight. These are cosmetic but real signals of AI-assisted drafting that was not fully edited.

strengths: Active rejection of legacy config keys ('copulas', 'cql_pattern') prevents silent fallback to old behaviour
Single backend (BlackLab) with no old executor classes remaining — no dead parallel codepaths
No @deprecated symbols still called by active code
No TODO/FIXME referencing migration or removal
incomplete_migration.issue_character: A small number of residual compatibility surface items remain — primarily the unused public indexDirectory() method and the legacy tag= attribute recognition — but none represent an active migration stalemate or deprecated-but-called symbol.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is substantially clean from an incomplete-migration perspective: no coexisting old/new API patterns, no active deprecated callsites, and migration guards that reject rather than transparently shim legacy formats. The two findings (unused public method, legacy tag= recognition) are minor loose-ends, not blocked migrations. A score around 88 reflects good completion with two small residues.

strengths: api/, config/, exploration/, and query/ packages each have a clear, consistent ownership boundary
Indexer logic is cleanly isolated under indexer/blacklab/
viz/ package correctly separates visualization concerns
package_organization.issue_character: The model/ and utils/ packages act as catch-alls that accumulate heterogeneous concerns, blurring ownership boundaries for result DTOs, visualization types, and generic data structures.
package_organization.score_rationale: The top-level package structure is sound and navigable for a project of this size. The model/ package mixes query result DTOs, visualization graph types, and numeric tuning records that could be split across result/, graph/, or the packages that own them. utils/ is a grab-bag with CQL-specific logic (CqlUtils) alongside a generic low-level data structure (LongIntHashMap). These are real but non-critical navigation friction points that a developer would encounter when searching for Edge or ExploreOptions. Score 76 reflects solid structure with two packages that benefit from scoping refinement.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface has clear, thorough Javadoc on all methods including error contracts
LogDiceCalculator.UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel is clearly documented, and callers use Double.isNaN() consistently
GrammarConfigLoader throws typed IOExceptions with actionable messages on all contract violations
RelationConfig.getDeprel() correctly annotated @Nullable and behaves accordingly
contract_coherence.issue_character: The dominant pattern is nullable-returning methods with non-nullable return type annotations — callers rely on runtime null checks rather than compile-time guarantees, introducing subtle contract lies that the type system cannot enforce.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has well-designed contracts in several places (QueryExecutor, LogDiceCalculator, GrammarConfigLoader validation). However, at least three public/package-private methods (requireParam, resolveRelationConfig, resolveExploreParams) return null without annotating the signature, and one method (fromReader) closes a resource the caller may still own. These lie at key API boundaries — HTTP parameter parsing and request handling — where a developer reading the signature would not expect null. Score 72 reflects solid intent with a recurring signature-lie pattern at the HTTP layer.

strengths: Guard clauses consistently return early for null/empty inputs across most methods
LogDiceCalculator has clean, explicit special-case handling (dice <= 0, headwordFreq <= 0) with no ambiguity
SemanticFieldExplorer phase-decomposition (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) makes each step's intent clear
LongIntHashMap uses documented EMPTY_KEY sentinel with explicit prohibition on storing that value
logic_clarity.issue_character: The clearest logic issues are a potential integer overflow in a limit calculation and a side-effect embedded inside a resource-close expression — both are subtle correctness bugs masquerading as clarity issues.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Most control flow in the codebase is clean and readable. Two concrete issues stand out: an int overflow risk in scoreHits() that is inconsistent with the correct (long) cast in the nearby executeBcqlQuery(), and a side-effect inside a finally close expression in ConlluConverter. The null propagation via empty(null) and the misleading indexFile-given-directory are lower-severity clarity concerns. Score 74 reflects good overall clarity with a few concrete defects that a code reader would need to reason carefully through.

strengths: BlackLabSnippetParser consistently uses @Nullable from jspecify on all methods that can return null
RelationConfig uses Optional<RelationType> correctly to model absence rather than nullable returns
Java's static type system catches the vast majority of type mismatches at compile time
LogDiceCalculator uses a UNCOMPUTABLE constant (Double.NaN) rather than nullable Double, providing a type-safe sentinel
type_safety.issue_character: The codebase has adopted @Nullable annotations for the query/snippet layer but has not consistently applied them to the API handler layer, where several package-private methods return null without annotation.
type_safety.score_rationale: Java's static typing provides a strong baseline. The codebase demonstrates awareness of nullability annotations (jspecify is already imported and used correctly in BlackLabSnippetParser and RelationConfig). The gap is in the HTTP handler layer where three methods that can return null lack @Nullable annotations, creating a systematic violation at the request-parsing boundary. Score 75 reflects strong compile-time guarantees with an inconsistent application of nullability annotations at one layer.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer phase-decomposition into private helper methods (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) is clean and well-named
CollocateQueryHelper correctly encapsulates the shared collocate-search and ranking logic as a package-private helper
ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers are properly split by domain concern
BlackLabQueryExecutor correctly delegates all heavy lifting to CollocateQueryHelper, keeping itself thin
design_coherence.issue_character: The key design issue is that a shared utility (collectFrequenciesFromGroups) exists for exactly the pattern executeSurfacePattern() needs, but that method re-implements the pattern inline — an inconsistency that will likely diverge over time.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The overall design is coherent: clear handler/service/query layering, good phase decomposition, appropriate delegation. The main defects are a bypass of the shared frequency-collection utility in executeSurfacePattern() and repeated parameter-parsing boilerplate across all four exploration handler methods. These are real design coherence gaps — one creates code that must be maintained in two places, the other makes handlers hard to read at a glance. Score 68 reflects good structural bones with two concrete repetition problems.

strengths: No module-level side effects — no I/O, no DB connections, no network at class load
Dependency injection throughout: BlackLabQueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader, and SemanticFieldExplorer are all constructed and passed through constructors
GrammarConfigLoader reads system property at call time (not import time), making it testable
Proper AutoCloseable and try-with-resources patterns for resource management
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The initialization concerns are mild: two hardcoded static constants embed grammar and CORS assumptions that should be configurable, and a mutable reference in a shutdown hook uses an array idiom where a cleaner alternative exists.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase has clean Java initialization: no circular dependencies, no boot-order surprises, and all stateful resources are constructed explicitly via DI. The main issues are the CORS constant and the grammar-tag constants baked into static fields — these are configuration values that cannot be changed without recompilation. The shutdown-hook array hack is a minor idiom concern. Score 83 reflects solid initialization hygiene with a few hardcoded static values that limit testability and configurability.

strengths: Core utilities (CqlUtils, LogDice, LongIntHashMap) have dedicated unit tests
Grammar config loading and relation validation are well tested via GrammarConfigLoaderTest and RelationsValidationTest
The complex snippet parsing and BCQL query logic (BlackLabSnippetParserTest, BCQLParserTest) are covered
SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator — the most algorithmically complex classes — have tests
test_strategy.issue_character: The gap is in the data pipeline (indexer, converter) and the infrastructure layer (server routing, CLI), where untested code has complex logic and high consequence of failure.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test suite is better than average for a project of this size: most business logic and query-execution paths are covered. The significant gap is in the indexer and converter (ConlluConverter, BlackLabConllUIndexer), which are the entry point for all corpus data and contain non-trivial logic (MWT token handling, close-with-counter side effect, error continuation). A bug in these files corrupts the entire index silently. The absence of RadialPlot and Main CLI tests is a lower priority but adds to the gap. Score 63 reflects strong coverage of the query/exploration domain with a notable absence at the data-ingestion boundary.

strengths: HTTP method discipline: GET for all read endpoints, POST for state-affecting or large-body endpoints (/api/bcql, /api/visual/radial)
Consistent JSON error envelope: all errors use {"error": "message"} shape via HttpApiUtils.sendError()
Consistent 'status: ok' field in all success responses
CORS handling is uniform across all endpoints via registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The dominant coherence issue is parameter naming asymmetry across the semantic-field endpoint family — 'seed' vs 'seeds', 'nouns_per' available in one but not the other — which creates inconsistent caller experience for endpoints that are conceptually related.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The REST API has several positive consistency traits (error envelope, status field, HTTP method discipline). However, the semantic-field endpoint family is visibly incoherent: similar operations have different parameter names ('seed' vs 'seeds'), one endpoint exposes a parameter ('nouns_per') that its sibling intentionally omits with a code comment rather than returning a 400 for it. The CORS constant prevents any deployment customisation. The absence of machine-readable API documentation means callers discover contracts by trial and error. Score 60 reflects decent baseline consistency undermined by a notably incoherent endpoint family and hardcoded infrastructure.

strengths: Loopback-only binding provides strong network-level isolation — the tool is not reachable from other machines by default
CORS restriction limits browser-origin access to a single known frontend
No mixed auth state: there are no endpoints with auth guards that protect some resources but not others
CqlUtils.escapeForRegex() provides partial lemma injection mitigation
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The authorization model is 'trust the local user completely' — consistent for a single-user local tool, but the /api/bcql arbitrary-query endpoint has no rate limiting or input-size controls, which would matter if the tool were ever deployed non-locally.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: For a local corpus analysis tool bound to loopback, consistent no-auth is the appropriate design: there is only one user (the person who started the server), and the loopback binding provides OS-level isolation. The consistency is complete — no endpoints have auth that their siblings lack. The deduction comes from two concerns: (1) the /api/bcql endpoint with its arbitrary-query power has no length limit or rate limiting, and (2) there is no mechanism to enable auth when deploying non-locally, so the tool cannot be safely deployed in a shared environment without code changes. Score 80 reflects appropriate consistency for the stated use case with limited defensive depth.

issues.summary: api layer couples all handlers to com.sun.net.httpserver (JDK-internal API), increasing replacement cost
issues.suggestion: Introduce a thin HttpContext abstraction (e.g. a record wrapping URI, method, body InputStream, and a response writer callback) in the api package. Have HttpApiUtils operate on that abstraction. This isolates com.sun.net.httpserver to WordSketchApiServer alone and makes handlers unit-testable.
evidence: All four api-layer classes import com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange directly
HttpApiUtils.sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, sendOptionsResponse, requireMethod, requireParam all accept HttpExchange
WordSketchApiServer.registerHandler signature includes com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer and HttpHandler as parameters

issues.summary: RelationType and PosGroup live in config but carry domain semantics consumed by query, api, and exploration packages
issues.suggestion: Move RelationType and PosGroup to the model package where other domain vocabulary lives (QueryResults, ExploreOptions). The config package should contain only loading/parsing logic.
evidence: RelationType.SURFACE and RelationType.DEP are used in SketchHandlers, RelationConfig, and SemanticFieldExplorer — not just in config loading
PosGroup.fromString and PosGroup.getValue are called from RelationConfig.collocatePosGroup() which is called throughout the query and api layers
A pure config enum would only be used by the loader; domain enums like these belong in a model or domain package

issues.summary: config package owns RelationType and PosGroup domain enums that are consumed across api, query, and exploration subsystems
issues.suggestion: Move RelationType and PosGroup to the model package. Keep config package focused on loading and parsing logic only (GrammarConfigLoader, RelationConfig).
evidence: PosGroup.fromString and getValue are called from RelationConfig.collocatePosGroup(), SketchHandlers, and SemanticFieldExplorer
RelationType.SURFACE and RelationType.DEP are used in SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType and handleFullSketchForType — not only config loading
model package already holds domain vocabulary (QueryResults, ExploreOptions, Edge); domain enums are split inconsistently

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader mixes file-loading responsibility with runtime query service (holds index, exposes getRelation accessor)
issues.suggestion: Extract the loaded state into a GrammarConfig value object (immutable, no I/O). Let GrammarConfigLoader be a pure factory that returns GrammarConfig. Consumers receive GrammarConfig, not the loader.
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader constructor reads JSON from disk and builds relationsById HashMap used at query time
getRelation(String id) and getRelations() serve as a runtime service registry, not just post-load access
getVersion() suggests the loader is also serving as a configuration manifest at runtime

issues.summary: requireNextArg has two consecutive Javadoc comment blocks — draft and final version coexist
issues.suggestion: Remove the earlier draft Javadoc block ('Returns {@code args[i]}, exiting with a helpful message...'). Retain only the fuller second block.
evidence: Lines immediately before requireNextArg contain '/** Returns {@code args[i]}...' (short draft) followed by '/** Returns the next argument...' (full replacement) with no intervening code
Only one Javadoc block should precede a method declaration; the preceding block is silently discarded by Javadoc tooling, leaving the draft as dead comment noise

issues.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser mixes get-prefix naming (extractLemmaFromMatch) with plain-verb naming (findLabelPosition) for similar static utility methods
issues.suggestion: Rename findLabelPosition to extractLabelPosition (or labelPositionOf) to align with the 'extract' family. Review extractPlainTextTokenAt for potential rename to extractTokenAt for brevity.
evidence: extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, extractCollocateLemma use 'extract' prefix
findLabelPosition uses 'find' prefix for what is effectively another extraction/lookup operation
extractHeadword uses 'extract' but extractPlainTextTokenAt departs to a longer descriptive style inconsistent with peers

issues.summary: handleBcqlQueryPost sends HTTP 400 for all non-IOException exceptions, misclassifying server errors as client errors
issues.suggestion: Narrow the catch to specific expected client-error exceptions (e.g. InvalidQuery from BlackLab, fastjson2 JSONException). Let unexpected RuntimeExceptions propagate to the wrapHandler backstop which correctly sends 500.
evidence: catch (Exception e) block sends status 400 with 'BCQL query error: ' + e.getMessage()
BlackLab InvalidQuery and JSON parse exceptions are plausible causes — InvalidQuery is client error, but NullPointerException or index corruption exceptions should be 500
The parallel handler handleVisualRadial sends 500 for Exception, making this an inconsistency across sibling handlers

issues.summary: findCollocations wraps IllegalArgumentException in IOException, conflating programming errors with I/O failures
issues.suggestion: Let IllegalArgumentException propagate unwrapped from findCollocations. Callers can catch it explicitly if they need to produce user-facing messages. Alternatively, validate the pattern before calling buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution and throw IllegalArgumentException directly from the public method signature.
evidence: catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { throw new IOException('Invalid CQL pattern...', e); } in findCollocations
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution throws IllegalArgumentException for patterns not starting with '[' — this is a programming contract violation, not an I/O failure
Callers that catch IOException will silently swallow what is actually an argument validation bug

issues.summary: LEMMA_ATTR_ANY name does not communicate that it also accepts single-quoted values and is case-insensitive, unlike LEMMA_ATTR
issues.suggestion: Rename LEMMA_ATTR_ANY to LEMMA_ATTR_RELAXED or LEMMA_ATTR_QUOTED (to signal it accepts both quote styles). Add a brief inline comment on the constant declaration explaining the two differences from LEMMA_ATTR.
evidence: LEMMA_ATTR = Pattern.compile("lemma=\"([^\"]+)\"")
LEMMA_ATTR_ANY = Pattern.compile("lemma=[\"']([^\"']+)[\"']", Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE)
The difference is quote style (double-only vs double-or-single) and case sensitivity — 'ANY' suggests broader match but does not hint at these specific axes

issues.summary: findLabelPosition return value (1-based token position) is ambiguous — 'position' could mean character offset, label number, or token index
issues.suggestion: Rename to findLabelTokenIndex or tokenIndexForLabel and update the caller variable to collocateTokenIndex. Add a brief Javadoc clarifying that the returned int is a 1-based token slot count within the BCQL pattern.
evidence: findLabelPosition(String pattern, int label) returns an int described only as 'The 1-based position of the token' in the extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition Javadoc
CQL patterns use 'position' to mean token slot; BlackLab's concordance API uses position to mean corpus position — the name collides with both
Caller in BlackLabQueryExecutor: int collocatePos = BlackLabSnippetParser.findLabelPosition(bcqlPattern, 2) — variable named 'collocatePos' which could also be read as character position

issues.summary: ExploreOptions.nounsPerSeed is unused and semantically inapplicable in multi-seed mode, making the options bag carry a conditional nullable field
issues.suggestion: Either use Optional<Integer> for nounsPerSeed in ExploreOptions to make the conditionality explicit, or introduce two separate options records: SingleSeedOptions (with nounsPerSeed) and MultiSeedOptions (without). This makes invalid states unrepresentable.
evidence: ExploreOptions record: int topCollocates, int nounsPerSeed, double minLogDice, int minShared, boolean includeExamples
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti comment: 'nouns_per intentionally not supported in multi-seed mode — seeds parameter is required instead'
Multi-seed call path creates ExploreOptions with nounsPerSeed but the value is never used by SemanticFieldExplorer in that path

issues.summary: handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType in SketchHandlers share identical outer loop structure but differ only in response building, suggesting a missing abstraction for the shared iteration
issues.suggestion: Extract a functional interface or a BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> responseBuilder parameter. A shared private method can own the iteration loop, with the response-builder lambda supplied by each call site.
evidence: Both methods iterate grammarConfig.getRelations(), check relationType, call executeAndFormatCollocations, catch IOException|RuntimeException identically, build a response Map with identical keys (status, lemma, relations, errors)
The only difference is: surface path uses collocations list directly, dep path calls buildRelationResponse and adds 'type':'dependency'
This duplication means any change to error handling or response envelope must be made in both methods

issues.summary: com.sun.net.httpserver (JDK-internal) used pervasively in api package with no abstraction, risking breakage on JDK version changes
issues.suggestion: Consider replacing com.sun.net.httpserver with a small but stable HTTP library (e.g. Undertow, Javalin, or the JDK's com.sun.net.httpserver accessed via the public jdk.httpserver module if targeting JDK 18+). In the interim, isolate all HttpExchange references to WordSketchApiServer and introduce a minimal HttpContext abstraction in the api package.
evidence: import com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange appears in all four api-package classes
com.sun.net.httpserver is not part of the java.* or javax.* public API; it is accessible on current JDKs but is subject to encapsulation warnings and potential removal
No try-with-module-access or --add-exports flags observed in CLAUDE.md build documentation

issues.summary: fastjson2 used for JSON serialisation where Jackson or Gson would be the standard Java ecosystem choice
issues.suggestion: Consider migrating to Jackson (com.fasterxml.jackson.databind) which is battle-tested, widely understood, and has better integration with Java records. The change is contained to the api package serialisation helpers.
evidence: import com.alibaba.fastjson2.JSON, JSONObject, JSONArray in SketchHandlers.java and HttpApiUtils.java
fastjson (v1) had numerous CVEs; fastjson2 is a rewrite but shares the brand, causing security scanner false positives and contributor hesitation
Jackson is the de-facto standard for Java JSON processing and would require no additional onboarding explanation

issues.summary: ConlluConverter uses writer==null as a lifecycle state flag across a nested try-finally, obscuring open/close semantics
issues.suggestion: Extract chunk lifecycle into a small ChunkWriter helper class or use a cleaner state variable (boolean chunkOpen or an AtomicReference). Alternatively, restructure with a dedicated openNextChunk helper that encapsulates the close-previous logic.
evidence: BufferedWriter writer = null declared before the while loop; openChunk called only when writer==null AND a new sentence begins
The finally block closes writer if != null, then increments chunks — this null-check is a state machine encoded as a nullable reference
Reassignment writer = null after close is absent; the sentencesInChunk >= sentencesPerChunk branch calls writer.close() and then sets writer = null implicitly by not reassigning

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates accepts a nullable posMap parameter, spreading null-handling across all call sites
issues.suggestion: Provide an overload buildAndRankCollocates(freqMap, headwordFreq, minLogDice, maxResults) without posMap, and remove the nullable parameter from the main signature. Callers without POS data call the no-posMap overload.
evidence: buildAndRankCollocates(freqMap, null, headwordFreq, minLogDice, maxResults) called in executeSurfacePattern with explicit null
collectDependencyFrequencies calls buildAndRankCollocates with a populated lemmaPosMap
The null path means 'no POS data available' — this optionality should be expressed as Optional<Map<String,String>> or an overloaded method without the posMap parameter

issues.summary: handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType in SketchHandlers duplicate the relation-iteration and response-envelope orchestration
issues.suggestion: Introduce a private iterateRelations method that accepts a RelationType and a BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> response-builder. The two public paths supply different builders but share the loop, error handling, and response envelope construction.
evidence: Both methods iterate grammarConfig.getRelations(), check relationType, call executeAndFormatCollocations, catch IOException|RuntimeException with the same warning log, accumulate to byRelation and relationErrors maps, then build a Map response with 'status', 'lemma', 'relations', 'errors' keys
The difference is: surface adds collocations list, dep calls buildRelationResponse and adds 'type':'dependency'
A new relation-level error-handling strategy would require changes in both methods

issues.summary: executeSurfacePattern inlines its own HitGroups iteration instead of using the shared collectFrequenciesFromGroups helper
issues.suggestion: Pass a lemma-extractor lambda to collectFrequenciesFromGroups that uses extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition(identity, collocatePos) for the surface path. This unifies all three HitGroups iteration paths through a single shared method.
evidence: collectFrequenciesFromGroups is called in findCollocations and collectDependencyFrequencies via a Function<String,String> lemma extractor
executeSurfacePattern contains its own for (HitGroup group : groups) loop with inline extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition logic rather than delegating to the shared helper
The two iteration loops are near-identical in structure, meaning any HitGroups iteration fix (e.g. empty-identity guard) must be made in both

issues.summary: requireNextArg has two consecutive Javadoc comment blocks — an unresolved draft immediately preceding the final version
issues.suggestion: Delete the first shorter Javadoc block. Retain only the complete second block with the Caution note about System.exit.
evidence: First block: '/** Returns {@code args[i]}, exiting with a helpful message if {@code i} is out of bounds. */'

Second block immediately follows: '/** Returns the next argument from {@code args} at index {@code i}...

Caution...*/'
The first block is a draft that was superseded and should have been deleted; Javadoc tooling silently discards it, leaving noise

issues.summary: Several Javadoc blocks restate the method name or field name without adding domain insight
issues.suggestion: Remove or replace Javadoc on handleFullDependencySketch with a comment explaining the dispatch rationale (why it delegates to handleDepSketchForType). Remove the QueryResults constructor comment. The UNKNOWN_POS comment could note the contract: callers should check for this value before displaying POS information.
evidence: handleFullDependencySketch Javadoc: 'Handle full dependency sketch request. Returns all dependency relations for the given lemma.' — identical to what the method name already communicates
QueryResults private constructor Javadoc: 'Utility class - prevent instantiation' — standard boilerplate that adds nothing beyond the private modifier
WordSketchResult.UNKNOWN_POS field Javadoc restates 'Sentinel for missing POS information returned by the tagger' — the constant name UNKNOWN_POS already communicates this fully

issues.summary: BlackLabConllUIndexer.indexDirectory() is a public method with no callsite in main code
issues.suggestion: If the method is not part of a public library API, make it private or remove it. If it is intentionally public for future use, add a Javadoc note explaining that.
evidence: Method defined at line ~94 but grep finds no callers in src/main/java; Main.java calls only indexFile()

issues.summary: RelationConfig retains recognition of legacy tag= POS attribute alongside current xpos=
issues.suggestion: Audit grammar JSON files to confirm tag= is no longer used. If confirmed unused, remove the tag= branch from posGroupForPrefix() and update the related comments.
evidence: posGroupForPrefix() recognises both 'xpos=' (current) and 'tag=' (legacy format per comment at line 154)
If no grammar files use tag= anymore, this is dead shim code

issues.summary: model/ mixes result DTOs, visualization graph types, and tuning config records
issues.suggestion: Staged reorg: (1) Move ExploreOptions to exploration/ alongside SemanticFieldExplorer; (2) Move Edge and RelationEdgeType to viz/ or exploration/ (they are only used by ExplorationResult.buildEdges and ComparisonResult.buildEdges); (3) Validate with: grep -r 'import.*model\.Edge\|import.*model\.RelationEdge\|import.*model\.ExploreOptions' src/main/java/ to find and update all import statements.
evidence: ExploreOptions (tuning knobs for SemanticFieldExplorer) and RelationEdgeType (graph edge classification) have no cohesive relationship to query DTOs like QueryResults
Edge and RelationEdgeType are visualization graph types that would more naturally live in viz/ or exploration/model/

issues.summary: utils/ mixes corpus-specific CQL/scoring utilities with a general-purpose data structure
issues.suggestion: Move LongIntHashMap to a new collections/ package or to the package that most heavily uses it. Alternatively, keep utils/ but document its scope explicitly. Run: grep -r 'import.*utils\.LongIntHashMap' src/main/java/ to identify the primary consumer and consider co-locating it there.
evidence: LongIntHashMap is a general-purpose open-addressing hash map with no dependency on corpus or CQL concerns
CqlUtils is CQL-language specific and tightly coupled to the grammar/query layer

issues.summary: HttpApiUtils.requireParam() returns String but can return null — signature lies
issues.suggestion: Add @Nullable annotation to the return type: 'public static @Nullable String requireParam(...)'. Import org.jspecify.annotations.Nullable (already used elsewhere in the codebase).
evidence: Method body: 'if (v.isEmpty()) { sendError(...); return null; }' but return type is 'String' not '@Nullable String'
Callers in ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers guard against null, proving they know it can be null

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig() returns RelationConfig but returns null on failure
issues.suggestion: Add @Nullable annotation: 'private @Nullable RelationConfig resolveRelationConfig(...)'. Same fix for resolveExploreParams().
evidence: Method body returns null when relation is unknown or misconfigured
All three callers check 'if (resolvedConfig == null) return' before using the result

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader() closes the Reader passed by the caller, violating resource-ownership convention
issues.suggestion: Either document clearly that fromReader() always closes the reader and callers must not close it again, or stop closing it inside the method and let the caller manage lifecycle. The simplest fix is to remove the try-with-resources around reader and instead read it with a plain while loop.
evidence: Method body: 'try (reader) { ... }' — explicitly closes the reader
Javadoc example shows 'try (Reader r = new StringReader(...)) { GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader(r); }' — caller is also managing the resource, risking double-close

issues.summary: Main.requireNextArg() has two successive Javadoc comment blocks — incomplete documentation migration
issues.suggestion: Remove the first, shorter Javadoc block; retain the detailed second block.
evidence: Two /** ... */ comment blocks appear before requireNextArg() — the first is a brief one-liner stub, the second is the detailed replacement
Only the second block is read by javadoc tools; the first is invisible but creates confusion in the source

issues.summary: scoreHits() computes maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR without (long) cast, risking integer overflow
issues.suggestion: Change to: 'int limit = (int) Math.min((long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, records.size());' — mirrors the correct pattern already used in executeBcqlQuery().
evidence: scoreHits(): 'int limit = Math.min(maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, records.size())' — both operands are int, so multiplication overflows if maxResults > Integer.MAX_VALUE / 3 (~715M)
executeBcqlQuery() nearby correctly casts: 'Math.min(hits.size(), (long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR)' — the inconsistency shows the risk was known at the call site but not applied consistently

issues.summary: ConlluConverter increments the chunks counter inside a resource-close expression in a finally block
issues.suggestion: Separate the increment from the close: 'if (writer != null) { writer.close(); chunks++; }' — or use try-finally without combining close and increment.
evidence: finally block: 'if (writer != null) { try (BufferedWriter toClose = writer) { chunks++; } }' — the chunks++ side effect is buried inside the try-with-resources close expression
If the writer.close() throws, chunks is still incremented, producing an off-by-one in the return value

issues.summary: BlackLabConllUIndexer.indexFile() is named for files but called with a directory path in Main.java
issues.suggestion: Rename indexFile() to indexPath() or indexFileOrDirectory() to reflect actual usage, or add a parameter check and delegate to indexDirectory() internally.
evidence: Main.java: 'indexer.indexFile(wplTempDir.toString())' — wplTempDir is created with Files.createTempDirectory()
indexFile() has a Files.exists() check that would pass for directories, and delegates to indexer.index(path.toFile()) which BlackLab handles for directories — so it works, but the name misleads

issues.summary: exploreByPattern() passes null to ExplorationResult.empty(seed) when seed is null
issues.suggestion: Change the guard to: 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) return ExplorationResult.empty("");' — or add a @NonNull precondition and let callers guarantee non-null input.
evidence: 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) return ExplorationResult.empty(seed)' — when seed is null, ExplorationResult.empty(null) stores null as the seed field
Callers of exploreByPattern() would receive a result with null seed, which could cause NPE if they call result.getSeed() on the returned object

issues.summary: HttpApiUtils.requireParam() return type lacks @Nullable despite documented null return
issues.suggestion: Add @Nullable to return type: 'public static @Nullable String requireParam(...)'. org.jspecify.annotations.Nullable is already imported in nearby files (e.g., BlackLabSnippetParser).
evidence: Return type declared as 'String' but method contains 'return null' when parameter is missing
All callers guard against null: 'if (seed == null) return;' — proving callers know the contract but type system cannot enforce it

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig() and resolveExploreParams() return non-annotated types but return null
issues.suggestion: Annotate both methods with @Nullable return type. Alternatively, consider throwing an exception and unifying error handling at the call site.
evidence: resolveRelationConfig() declared as 'private RelationConfig ...' but contains 'return null'
resolveExploreParams() declared as 'private ExploreParams ...' but contains 'return null'
Both callers check for null before use

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates() accepts a nullable posMap without @Nullable annotation
issues.suggestion: Add @Nullable to the posMap parameter: 'buildAndRankCollocates(Map<String, Long> freqMap, @Nullable Map<String, String> posMap, ...)'.
evidence: Method signature: 'List<...> buildAndRankCollocates(Map<String, Long> freqMap, Map<String, String> posMap, ...)' — posMap can be null
Body: 'String pos = posMap != null ? posMap.getOrDefault(...) : ...' — null check is present but the parameter is not annotated @Nullable
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern() passes null explicitly: 'buildAndRankCollocates(freqMap, null, headwordFreq, ...)'

issues.summary: executeSurfacePattern() reimplements frequency collection inline instead of using collectFrequenciesFromGroups()
issues.suggestion: Refactor executeSurfacePattern() to use collectFrequenciesFromGroups() by passing an appropriate lemma extractor lambda: 'identity -> BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition(identity, collocatePos)' with a fallback chained in. This unifies the frequency-collection path.
evidence: collectFrequenciesFromGroups() exists at line ~170 with a Function<String, String> lemmaExtractor parameter
executeSurfacePattern() contains a custom for loop over groups with its own collocate-extraction logic (extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition with plain-text fallback)
The two paths differ only in extraction strategy — exactly the kind of variation collectFrequenciesFromGroups() was designed to handle via its extractor parameter

issues.summary: All four ExplorationHandlers methods repeat the same parameter-parsing preamble without shared extraction
issues.suggestion: Extract a 'parseBaseExploreRequest(exchange)' record or a command object that bundles the common params and short-circuits with null on validation failure, reducing the four preambles to a single call each.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison each call parseQueryParams, then requireParam, then resolveRelationConfig, then resolveExploreParams in the same order
resolveRelationConfig() and resolveExploreParams() are already extracted — but the outer pattern is repeated 3-4 times

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer mixes two algorithmically distinct flows (single-seed vs multi-seed) in one class
issues.suggestion: Consider extracting MultiSeedExplorer as a separate class (parallel to CollocateProfileComparator) so each algorithm has a focused class. SemanticFieldExplorer would then only contain the single-seed exploration path. This mirrors how CollocateProfileComparator was already separated.
evidence: exploreByPattern() (single-seed): multi-phase, builds DiscoveredNoun list by reverse-lookup through collocates — ~120 lines with 4 private helper methods
exploreMultiSeed() (multi-seed): fetches per-seed collocates directly, intersects, maps seeds into DiscoveredNoun without reverse lookup — ~60 lines with a completely different algorithm
The two methods share only the QueryExecutor dependency and the ExplorationResult return type

issues.summary: HttpApiUtils.CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN is a hardcoded static constant with no runtime override mechanism
issues.suggestion: Read the value from a system property with a default: 'static final String CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = System.getProperty("cors.allow.origin", "http://localhost:3000")'. Or make it an instance field passed into HttpApiUtils methods. The system-property approach requires minimal refactoring.
evidence: CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = 'http://localhost:3000' — hardcoded at compile time
Comment: 'Restrict to specific origins in production. Change this value (or make it configurable) when deploying'
No system property, environment variable, or constructor parameter allows changing this value

issues.summary: NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT and ADJECTIVE_PATTERN are static constants hardcoding English Penn Treebank xpos tags
issues.suggestion: Consider deriving these patterns from the loaded grammar config (which already encodes collocate POS groups per relation) rather than hardcoding them. The RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() method already provides this abstraction — use it consistently instead of the static constants.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer: 'private static final String NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]"' — English-only
CollocateProfileComparator: 'private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]"' — English-only
These are used as reverse-lookup patterns in exploration; different corpora or languages would require different tags

issues.summary: Shutdown hook captures server reference via WordSketchApiServer[] array instead of AtomicReference
issues.suggestion: Replace with: 'AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> serverRef = new AtomicReference<>(); ... serverRef.set(server); ... if (serverRef.get() != null) serverRef.get().stop();'. Alternatively, move shutdown logic into WordSketchApiServer.start() itself.
evidence: 'WordSketchApiServer[] serverHolder = new WordSketchApiServer[1];' used to allow mutation in a lambda/inner class
The array is written exactly once: 'serverHolder[0] = server' — an AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> or a simple final wrapper class would make the intent clearer

issues.summary: ConlluConverter has no tests despite complex MWT-skipping and sentence-boundary logic
issues.suggestion: Write unit tests for: (1) basic sentence conversion, (2) MWT token skipping, (3) sentence count across chunk boundaries, (4) empty corpus input, (5) corpus with only MWT tokens. Use a small in-memory test CoNLL-U string written to a temp file.
evidence: No ConlluConverterTest found in src/test/
The method convertConlluToWplChunks handles: multi-word tokens (MWT_OR_EMPTY regex), sentence boundaries (<s>/<s>), chunk file rotation, and a close-with-counter side effect in finally
The return value long[] { sentences, tokens, chunks } is used by Main.java for progress reporting — incorrect counts would go undetected

issues.summary: BlackLabConllUIndexer has no tests for its error-continuation and close-ordering logic
issues.suggestion: Add integration tests using a small test corpus file. Test: (1) successful indexing with accurate document/token counts, (2) error counting when a malformed file is present. A BlackLabDummyTest already exists — check if it can be extended with a real small-corpus test.
evidence: No BlackLabConllUIndexerTest found in src/test/
The IndexListener.errorOccurred() continues after errors — this means a corrupt file silently produces a partial index
The close() method has a complex try/catch that throws IOException on finalisation failure — untested failure path

issues.summary: RadialPlot.toSVG() has coordinate math and conditional rendering with no tests
